does: This paper analyzes reward learning in RLHF through a social-choice lens, revealing a fundamental tradeoff between representation capacity and rationalizability. The authors prove that when reward models use learned representations φ(x,y) followed by a scalar head, they cannot simultaneously maximize representation richness and ensure that the resulting scalar reward avoids preference cycles (Condorcet cycles) across all annotators. They formalize this as a quantitative tradeoff: improving the representation's ability to distinguish responses necessarily increases the likelihood of cyclic preferences in the pooled annotator judgments. The work translates classical impossibility results from social choice theory—which assume a fixed set of alternatives—into a modern setting where the representation itself defines which responses are treated as distinguishable.
connects: This work builds on two converging research directions. First, it extends recent theoretical analyses of RLHF as a social-choice aggregation problem, moving beyond the classical assumption of a pre-enumerated finite set of alternatives. Second, it engages with foundational social-choice theory, particularly Arrow's impossibility theorem and the concept of Condorcet cycles, applying these to the reward-learning setting. The paper responds to the gap between social-choice theory's traditional framework and modern deep-learning pipelines where learned representations determine the effective alternative space. It bridges decades of work on preference aggregation with contemporary RLHF practices, recognizing that the representation layer acts as a choice of which comparisons the reward model can meaningfully evaluate.
enables: This framework opens several research directions for designing more robust reward-learning systems. Understanding the representation-rationalizability tradeoff allows practitioners to make informed choices about representation capacity during RLHF training, potentially trading off expressiveness for consistency. It suggests new objectives for representation learning that account for preference-structure constraints rather than purely supervised signal matching. The work points toward developing representation schemes that reduce Condorcet cycles through careful architectural choices or regularization. It also motivates further theoretical analysis of how representation learning, preference aggregation, and reward consistency interact, potentially leading to new algorithms that explicitly optimize the tradeoff or approximate rationalizability when perfect consistency is impossible.
